metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency edge to story E, and state the read-layer contract this story relies on (at minimum: module path, function name, and return type) so the index route and its end-to-end test can be implemented without clarification.
evidence: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'
'a live end-to-end test starting the server on an ephemeral port and asserting the index returns HTTP 200 and lists a seeded audited ticket (test-design §3 server/CLI)'
ABSENCE: no import path, no function signature, no return schema, and no dependency edge to story E anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The index route implementation and the final live end-to-end AC both depend on 'the read layer from story E', but story E is neither defined in this plan, listed as a blocking dependency, nor given an API contract (import path, function signatures, return schema). An executor cannot implement `server.py`'s index route or write/pass the final AC without knowing what story E provides.
findings.location: Scope › `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; Acceptance Criteria › final bullet ('live end-to-end test … lists a seeded audited ticket')
scenarios: Executor reaches `server.py` implementation and cannot write the index route without knowing the read-layer API.
Final AC cannot be proven green if story E's read layer is not yet merged — the end-state is unreachable within this story alone.
Story is declared complete (all other ACs green) but the live end-to-end test is silently deferred, leaving the riskiest path (a real index page with real data) unexercised.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Dependencies` section: 'Story E must be merged first (or stub-merged with a known API). The index route calls `rebar.audit.read.list_audited_tickets() -> list[str]` (ticket IDs); the end-to-end test seeds one ticket via `<seeding mechanism>` before asserting HTTP 200 lists it.' Adjust the final AC's Oracle to reference the same import path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's intercept convention description: `help/audit.txt` already exists and `audit` IS in `known_subcommands()`; the plan must specify that `help/audit.txt` be updated to document the new `serve` subcommand, and that the `__init__.py` conditional guard (`and not argv[1] in ('--help', '-h')`) already handles the --help passthrough correctly.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt exists with content: 'Usage: rebar audit show <ticket_id> [--output json|text]...'
src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:483: `if argv and argv[0] == 'audit' and not (len(argv) >= 2 and argv[1] in ('--help', '-h')):` — the conditional guard exists precisely because audit.txt is present
src/rebar/_cli/_help.py:57-67: known_subcommands() auto-derives from *.txt files under help/, so audit IS already a known subcommand
Plan quote: 'This is the intercept convention: NO `help/*.txt` file and NO `known_subcommands` change'
findings.finding: The plan asserts the audit intercept follows a 'NO help/*.txt file and NO known_subcommands change' convention, but help/audit.txt already exists and audit is already in known_subcommands(). The existing __init__.py intercept (line 483) uses a conditional bypass `and not (len(argv) >= 2 and argv[1] in ('--help', '-h'))` specifically because the pinned help text handles --help. The plan's description of the intercept convention directly contradicts the current codebase implementation of the very intercept it is building on.
findings.location: Scope section — CLI paragraph: 'NO `help/*.txt` file and NO `known_subcommands` change'
scenarios: A developer reading the plan's intercept convention description and applying it literally to add `serve` would not update audit.txt — but audit.txt already exists and must be updated to document `serve`.

The plan's claim that intercepts 'deliberately are not' golden-captured is false for audit specifically — it already has a help/*.txt and appears in known_subcommands(), so the serve subcommand needs to appear in audit.txt.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plan text 'NO `help/*.txt` file and NO `known_subcommands` change' with: 'UPDATE `help/audit.txt` to add the `serve` subcommand synopsis (it already exists and registers `audit` in `known_subcommands()`; the existing conditional guard in `__init__.py:483` handles --help passthrough correctly — do not change that guard pattern).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's premise: `config.py` does NOT re-export every `_config_schema` class automatically (e.g., `IdentityConfig` is absent); confirm the plan explicitly states that `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` must be added as an explicit re-export line.
evidence: src/rebar/config.py:30-40: explicit re-exports are CompactConfig, EnsureConfig, JiraConfig, McpConfig, ReconcilerConfig, ScratchConfig, SyncConfig, TicketClarityConfig, TicketConfig, TrackerConfig, VerifyConfig — IdentityConfig is absent from the re-export list
src/rebar/_config_schema.py:318: IdentityConfig exists in _config_schema.py but has no matching `from rebar._config_schema import IdentityConfig as IdentityConfig` line in config.py
Plan quote: 'this module re-exports every `_config_schema` class'
findings.finding: The plan claims '`config.py` re-exports every `_config_schema` class', but `IdentityConfig` (and potentially others) is NOT explicitly re-exported from `config.py` — only CompactConfig, EnsureConfig, JiraConfig, McpConfig, ReconcilerConfig, ScratchConfig, SyncConfig, TicketClarityConfig, TicketConfig, TrackerConfig, and VerifyConfig are explicitly aliased. The blanket re-export claim is false, meaning `UiConfig` re-export is NOT guaranteed by convention alone and must be deliberate.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/config.py` bullet: 're-export `UiConfig` (this module re-exports every `_config_schema` class)'
scenarios: An implementer relying on the 'every class is re-exported' claim as a codebase invariant may assume UiConfig will appear automatically or that another class name-collision is safe — but the re-export is intentional per-class, and IdentityConfig's absence shows the 'every class' claim is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(this module re-exports every `_config_schema` class)' to '(add an explicit `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` line matching the pattern of the other per-class re-exports in lines 30–40)'

findings.finding: AC5 requires the server's index route to list 'a seeded audited ticket' by calling 'the read layer from story E', but (a) no dependency edge on story E is declared and (b) the interface (module path, function signature) of that read layer is not specified in this plan. If story E is in-flight or its interface changes, AC5 is unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: AC5 ('lists a seeded audited ticket') and Scope → server.py ('one index route using the read layer from story E')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Depends on story E (read layer). Expected interface: `from rebar.audit.store import list_audited_tickets() -> list[str]`. If story E is not yet merged, `server.py` uses a thin stub (`return []`) and AC5 seeds data via that stub.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section that the `audit` intercept already exists in `_cli/__init__.py:483`; specify that the implementation must EXTEND `_audit_commands.audit_cli` (or the existing arm) to dispatch `serve` rather than registering a new arm.
evidence: Plan: 'register `audit` as an INTERCEPT in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main (mirroring the workflow/llm/explain intercepts — an `if argv and argv[0] == "audit": return _audit(argv[1:])` arm)'

Live code: src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:483 — `if argv and argv[0] == "audit" and not (len(argv) >= 2 and argv[1] in ("--help", "-h")): from rebar._cli._audit_commands import audit_cli; return audit_cli(argv[1:])`
The audit intercept is already present and would shadow any new arm added below it
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section says to 'register `audit` as an INTERCEPT in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main`' as if creating it fresh, but the live codebase already contains an `audit` intercept at line 483 of that file (dispatching to `_audit_commands.audit_cli`). The 'serve' subcommand must be added by EXTENDING the existing `_audit_commands.py` (or the intercept arm), not by creating a new one. If an implementer follows the plan literally and adds a fresh intercept arm, they will produce a duplicate that never fires (Python if-chains hit the first match). The plan should clarify it is extending the existing intercept.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI: register `audit` as an INTERCEPT …'
scenarios: An implementer adds a second `if argv and argv[0] == 'audit'` arm below the existing one — it is dead code because the first arm fires first
An implementer replaces the existing arm but misses the `--help/-h` guard already in it, breaking `rebar audit --help` behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope bullet to: 'CLI: EXTEND the existing `audit` intercept arm in `_cli/__init__.py` and `_cli/_audit_commands.audit_cli` to dispatch `serve` in addition to `show`; do NOT add a second `if argv[0] == "audit"` arm (the existing arm at line 483 already owns the intercept slot).'

findings.finding: AC1's oracle ('a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server') does not verify the second half of the AC's own requirement: that the refusal message 'names the flag'. The message-content assertion is stated in prose but absent from the oracle.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC1 — 'refuses to start with a message naming the flag. Oracle: a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the oracle: 'a test asserting no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar`, the disabled path does not start a server, AND the stderr/stdout message contains "[ui] enabled".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the sequencing contradiction: either (a) explicitly stub or inline the read layer in this story so it truly ships independently first, or (b) declare story E as a prerequisite and remove the 'ships first' claim.
evidence: 'Establishing that surface … is the riskiest architectural piece … so it ships first as a thin vertical slice'
'one index route using the read layer from story E'
AC 5: 'asserting the index returns HTTP 200 and lists a seeded audited ticket'
findings.finding: The plan claims this story 'ships first as a thin vertical slice' (Why section) yet Scope/server.py says the index route uses 'the read layer from story E', and AC 5 requires seeding and listing audited tickets — work that depends on story E being available first. These two sections directly contradict each other on sequencing.
findings.location: Why section ('ships first as a thin vertical slice') vs. Scope / server.py ('one index route using the read layer from story E') and AC 5
scenarios: Implementer starts this story 'first' but cannot satisfy AC 5 or wire the index route without story E's read layer; they must either stub the read layer (undocumented) or wait for story E (contradicting the stated order).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Scope that for this story the index route may use a minimal stub (e.g., return an empty list) so the server scaffolding AC can be satisfied independently, with story E wiring in the real read layer in its own story.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify (or reconsider) wiring `UiConfig` into the core `Config` dataclass instead of using the `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` + `read_reserved_section` pattern that the analogous `llm` optional-extra section already uses.
evidence: `_config_schema.py` line 568: `_RESERVED_SECTIONS: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"llm", "snapshot"})` — the existing mechanism for optional-layer-owned config sections.
`_config_schema.py` lines 582-584: reserved sections are `continue`d out of `coerce_sparse` and NOT coerced into `Config` — they stay raw for the optional layer to consume.

The `llm` extra is architecturally analogous to `ui` (both are optional extras with their own config section), yet `llm` uses `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` while the plan adds `ui` to the core `Config` dataclass.
ABSENCE: The plan does not explain why `ui` should be a first-class stdlib-core Config field rather than a reserved section consumed by `rebar.audit.server` lazily.
findings.finding: The plan wires `UiConfig` directly into the core `Config` dataclass (adding it to `_SECTIONS`, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, and as `ui: UiConfig = field(default_factory=UiConfig)`) rather than using the established `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` pattern that the `llm` optional-extra section already uses — without justifying the architectural divergence.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/_config_schema.py — the FULL UiConfig wiring … (1) add a UiConfig dataclass … (2) add ui: UiConfig = field(default_factory=UiConfig) to the top-level Config dataclass; (3) register "ui": UiConfig in _SECTION_CLASSES; (4) add "ui": {"enabled": …} to the _SECTIONS coercer map'
scenarios: The core `Config` dataclass and its unit tests now carry a UI-shaped field even in a deployment with no `[ui]` section and no `ui` extra installed — coupling the always-imported stdlib core to an optional capability.

A future audit-layer config key (e.g. `ui.port`) must be added to both `_config_schema.py` (core) and `_optional.py`/`server.py` (optional layer), whereas the reserved-section pattern keeps all `ui.*` handling inside the optional layer.
The pattern divergence makes it harder for contributors to understand which extras are 'core-owned' vs 'optional-layer-owned' config sections.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"ui"` to `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` (alongside `"llm"` and `"snapshot"`). In `rebar.audit.server` (or a new `rebar.audit.config` module), read the raw `[ui]` table via `config.read_reserved_section("ui")` and coerce it locally — keeping `UiConfig` out of the always-imported core `Config` dataclass entirely. This matches the `llm` pattern and keeps the stdlib core clean.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `port: int = 8765` to `UiConfig` and wire a `--port` CLI flag so the listen port is configurable without code changes.
evidence: 'Default port 8765'
UiConfig dataclass only specifies `enabled: bool = False`; no `port` field.
serve(host, port) signature implies port is parameterized but the plan shows no CLI flag or config key wiring it.
findings.finding: The default port 8765 is mentioned as a hardcoded default, but no `--port` CLI flag or `UiConfig.port` config field is specified, leaving users with no recourse when port 8765 is already in use.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`
scenarios: Port 8765 is occupied by another process; the user has no flag to change it and must kill the other process or edit source.
CI environments running multiple rebar instances in parallel collide on the fixed port.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `port: int = 8765` to the `UiConfig` dataclass, register a `'port': lambda v, k: int(v)` coercer entry, and add `--port` to the `audit serve` argument parser, yielding env override `REBAR_UI_PORT`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a criterion (or extend AC 5) asserting that the server starts on port 8765 when no --port flag is supplied.
evidence: 'Default port 8765'
ABSENCE: no AC mentions port 8765 or asserts the default port value
findings.finding: The default port 8765 is a described deliverable (stated in What: 'Default port 8765') but no AC verifies it — neither the live end-to-end test AC nor any other criterion asserts the default port value.
findings.location: What section ('Default port 8765') vs. Acceptance Criteria (no port-default AC)
scenarios: Implementer ships a different default port (e.g. 8000) and all ACs still pass; the deliverable is silently wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 5: 'When no --port flag is supplied, the server defaults to port 8765; the end-to-end test verifies this by omitting --port and asserting the bound port.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or extend an AC to verify that `from rebar.config import UiConfig` succeeds and that `docs/config.md` contains an entry for `ui.enabled`.
evidence: 'src/rebar/config.py — re-export UiConfig (this module re-exports every _config_schema class); document the key in docs/config.md'
ABSENCE: no AC references config.py re-export or docs/config.md
findings.finding: The Scope section describes two deliverables — the `UiConfig` re-export in `src/rebar/config.py` and documentation in `docs/config.md` — but no AC verifies either of them.
findings.location: Scope / config.py bullet ('re-export UiConfig … document the key in docs/config.md') vs. Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: The re-export is omitted and downstream consumers cannot `from rebar.config import UiConfig`; the docs entry is never written. Both are undetected by the AC set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `serve()` catches `OSError` (EADDRINUSE) from uvicorn startup and exits with an actionable 'Port N is already in use' message rather than a raw traceback.
evidence: 'a `serve(host, port)` entry' — no mention of catching OSError/EADDRINUSE from uvicorn startup
ABSENCE: no error-handling guidance for the bind step in server.py or in the ACs
findings.finding: The plan does not address the port-already-in-use (EADDRINUSE) failure mode when uvicorn attempts to bind, leaving the user with an unhandled OS error rather than an actionable message.
findings.location: ## Scope / New `src/rebar/audit/server.py`
scenarios: Port 8765 is occupied; uvicorn raises OSError with a raw traceback instead of 'Port 8765 is already in use — try --port <other>'.
Automated test suites running in parallel leave stale listeners that cause flaky test failures with opaque errors.
findings.suggested_fix: In server.py, wrap the `uvicorn.run(...)` call in a try/except OSError block and re-raise as SystemExit with a message like: 'Cannot bind to {host}:{port} — address already in use. Try --port <other_port>.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a non-happy-path spec for port-already-in-use: define the error message (e.g. 'Port 8765 is in use; use --port to choose another') and add an AC or oracle for it.
evidence: 'Default port 8765' — plan names the port but gives no handling for bind failure
ABSENCE: no plan text addresses OSError/EADDRINUSE on startup
AC #5 only tests HTTP 200 on a successful bind (ephemeral port)
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior when the default port 8765 is already in use; uvicorn will raise an OSError with no user-friendly handling or fallback described.
findings.location: Scope — src/rebar/audit/server.py; AC #5
scenarios: User runs `rebar audit serve` while another process holds port 8765 — gets a raw traceback instead of a friendly message
CI or repeated invocations hit the same port
findings.suggested_fix: In server.py's serve() entry, wrap uvicorn.run() in a try/except OSError; catch EADDRINUSE specifically and print a friendly message suggesting --port, then SystemExit(1). Document the --port flag in Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the empty-state UX for the index page (e.g. a message like 'No audit data yet — run `rebar audit` to generate reports') and add an oracle asserting the empty-state response.
evidence: AC #5 specifies only the happy path: 'lists a seeded audited ticket'
ABSENCE: no plan text describes what the index renders when the read layer returns an empty collection
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the UX for the index page when there are zero audited tickets (empty state), leaving implementors to guess between a blank page, a placeholder message, or an error.
findings.location: Scope — src/rebar/audit/server.py index route; AC #5
scenarios: First-time user runs `rebar audit serve` before any audits have been run — sees a blank or broken page
Demo/onboarding scenario where the empty state is the first impression

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Register `'ui'` in `rebar._optional.EXTRAS` (with a probe module such as `'fastapi'` and a capability blurb) and replace the bespoke `except ModuleNotFoundError: sys.exit(...)` in the CLI handler with `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` — the same pattern already used for agents/eval/tracing/grounding.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke inline `try: ... except ModuleNotFoundError: sys.exit(...)` pattern for the missing-extra case in `server.py`, bypassing the already-established `rebar._optional.guard_import` / `require_extra` chokepoint that exists for exactly this purpose and is already wired for agents, eval, tracing, and grounding extras.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`: 'lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly
scenarios: The bespoke SystemExit message may diverge in wording from the standard `pip install 'nava-rebar[ui]'` format produced by `_install_hint`, creating an inconsistent UX across extras.
If `extra_installed` / `require_extra` are ever called for `ui` (e.g. in tests using the `block_extra` fixture), they silently return False/unknown rather than the registered probe.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_optional.py`, add `"ui": ("fastapi", "local read-only audit web UI (rebar audit serve)")` to `EXTRAS`. In `server.py`'s CLI handler, replace the inline try/except with `from rebar._optional import guard_import; guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` before the lazy FastAPI import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` to the Scope section as a file to update, documenting the new `rebar audit serve [--host HOST] [--port PORT]` usage alongside the existing `show` entry.
evidence: Plan states: 'This is the intercept convention: NO help/*.txt file and NO known_subcommands change'
`src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` exists and contains only `Usage: rebar audit show <ticket_id> [--output json|text]`

The existing `__init__.py` comment (line 478–482) confirms `audit` is a hybrid: it has BOTH pinned `audit.txt` help text AND an intercept arm — the plan's convention description does not match the actual `audit` setup
findings.finding: The plan states the audit intercept convention has 'NO help/*.txt file', but `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` already exists. The Scope section does not enumerate `audit.txt` as a file needing an update to document the new `serve` subcommand alongside the existing `show` entry.
findings.location: Scope section — CLI paragraph; src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt
scenarios: Implementer reads the plan's 'NO help/*.txt' statement and doesn't update audit.txt — `rebar help audit` and `rebar audit --help` then show only `show`, not `serve`
`rebar audit serve --help` may fall through to the generic help machinery and display stale text
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: '`src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` — update to add the `serve` usage line: `rebar audit serve [--host HOST] [--port PORT]` alongside the existing `show` line.' Also correct the CLI paragraph: `audit` is a HYBRID (pinned help text + intercept arm), not a pure intercept — the 'NO help/*.txt' convention does not apply to it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update the parent epic (2141) to add an index/listing page to its IN-scope items, or remove the index page from this leaf and move it to the sibling rendering story — the leaf should not deliver scope the parent's plan does not contain.
evidence: Parent epic 2141 IN scope: 'the optional web-server surface (subcommand, config flag, [ui] extra, lazy import, loopback binding) and the per-ticket audit page rendering' — no index/listing page mentioned.
Parent AC3: 'serves a ticket audit page showing the ticket details and the plan/completion/code panels with pass-1..4 detail' — a ticket-detail page, not a listing page.

Leaf What: 'serving a read-only INDEX page listing tickets that have audit data (each linking to a per-ticket page that the sibling rendering story fills in)' — explicitly new deliverable absent from parent.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces an index page (listing tickets that have audit data) that does not appear in the parent epic's scope or ACs. The parent's IN scope is 'the optional web-server surface … and the per-ticket audit page rendering'; it mentions no index/listing page. Delivering an index page is additive scope the parent does not contain.
findings.location: Leaf plan § What / § Scope / AC5 ('index returns HTTP 200 and lists a seeded audited ticket')
scenarios: The index page is a useful architectural step but the parent never calls for it; a reviewer enforcing strict G7 containment sees scope creep.
If the sibling story also ships an index page (or assumes the leaf will), the two stories conflict; the parent provides no anchor to resolve this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one bullet to parent epic 2141 Scope § IN: 'an index page listing tickets that have audit data (entry point for navigation)' — then the leaf is a clean subset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update parent epic 2141 to add the `--host` flag and non-loopback-warning behaviour to its IN-scope items and ACs, or remove `--host` / AC4 from this leaf (ship loopback-only binding as the parent specifies).
evidence: Parent epic 2141 IN scope: '(subcommand, config flag, [ui] extra, lazy import, loopback binding)' — loopback binding only, no --host flag.

Parent epic 2141 OUT scope: 'network exposure / multi-user auth' and 'actually exposing the service to a network (and any auth then required) is OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented'.
Parent ACs contain no --host flag test; the leaf's AC4 tests --host 0.0.0.0 behaviour that the parent never specifies.
Leaf 'Endpoint access contract': 'Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit --host flag that prints a warning to stderr' — mechanism the parent does not sanction.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a `--host` flag that permits non-loopback binding (with a stderr warning) and adds a dedicated AC (AC4) testing this behaviour. The parent's scope specifies loopback binding as the only IN-scope binding mode and classifies 'actually exposing the service to a network' as OUT of scope; the parent has no AC or scope item for a `--host` flag.
findings.location: Leaf plan § Endpoint access contract / § What / AC4 ('rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0 prints a non-loopback exposure warning to stderr before binding')
scenarios: A reader of the parent sees network exposure as OUT of scope; a --host flag that enables it (even with a warning) contradicts that boundary.
The leaf's AC4 tests an observable behaviour absent from the parent's acceptance criteria, making the leaf untestable against the parent's own success criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Register `'ui'` in `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` dict and gate the lazy import via `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')` rather than a bespoke `try/except ModuleNotFoundError + SystemExit`, matching every other extra's established pattern.
evidence: src/rebar/_optional.py defines `guard_import`, `require_extra`, and the `EXTRAS` registry — the repo's canonical chokepoint for turning a missing extra into an actionable install message.
The agents, eval, tracing, and grounding extras all route through `guard_import`/`require_extra`; none use a raw `try/except ModuleNotFoundError`.
The plan's AC3 says 'simulating ModuleNotFoundError on the lazy import' — testing the raw exception path rather than the `OptionalDependencyError` the guard infrastructure raises.
ABSENCE: The plan never mentions registering 'ui' in `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` dict or calling `guard_import`/`require_extra`.
findings.finding: The plan describes a raw `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit` to gate the lazy import, bypassing the established `_optional.py` infrastructure (`guard_import` / `require_extra` / `EXTRAS` dict) that every other optional extra already uses for exactly this purpose.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/server.py … lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" SystemExit on ModuleNotFoundError'
scenarios: A developer adds a second `ui`-extra-gated feature later and has to re-invent the gating pattern rather than calling `require_extra('ui')`.
The error message produced by the raw `SystemExit` drifts from the canonical `OptionalDependencyError` format, creating inconsistent UX across extras.
The AC3 oracle test exercises `ModuleNotFoundError` directly; if the implementation later switches to `guard_import`, the test silently loses coverage of the real code path.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_optional.py`, add `"ui": ("fastapi", "audit web UI (read-only local server)")` to `EXTRAS`. In `server.py` and the CLI handler, call `require_extra("ui")` (or `guard_import("fastapi", extra="ui")`) before the lazy import block, and let `OptionalDependencyError` propagate (or catch it and `sys.exit(1)` with `str(exc)` as the message). Update AC3's oracle to assert on `OptionalDependencyError` rather than raw `ModuleNotFoundError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section an explicit step to register `'ui': ('fastapi', '<blurb>')` in the `EXTRAS` dict in `src/rebar/_optional.py`, then use `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')` in the CLI handler rather than a raw `try/except ModuleNotFoundError + SystemExit`.
evidence: Plan: 'lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on `ModuleNotFoundError`'
src/rebar/_optional.py EXTRAS dict has keys: 'agents', 'eval', 'tracing', 'grounding' — 'ui' is absent
require_extra() raises ValueError for unknown extras: `if extra not in EXTRAS: raise ValueError(f'unknown extra {extra!r}')`
Established pattern in the codebase for all other extras is guard_import / require_extra, not raw SystemExit
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on `ModuleNotFoundError`' via a raw try/except in the CLI handler, but the established pattern in `src/rebar/_optional.py` is `guard_import(module, extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')`, which in turn requires `'ui'` to be registered in the `EXTRAS` dict. The plan's Scope section makes no mention of updating `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` dict. If `guard_import` is used without registering `'ui'`, `require_extra` raises `ValueError('unknown extra')` instead of the friendly install hint. If a raw `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit` is used instead, it bypasses the established guard mechanism and produces an inconsistent UX (different error format than the `agents`/`eval`/`tracing`/`grounding` extras).
findings.location: Scope section — 'New `src/rebar/audit/server.py` … lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on `ModuleNotFoundError`'
scenarios: Implementer uses `require_extra('ui')` → ValueError('unknown extra "ui"') because 'ui' is not in EXTRAS
Implementer uses raw try/except → works technically but produces an error message format inconsistent with every other extra in the codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope `_optional.py` bullet: 'add `"ui": ("fastapi", "the audit web UI (read-only local server)")` to the `EXTRAS` dict in `src/rebar/_optional.py`'; then change the server.py description to 'call `require_extra("ui")` at the top of the CLI handler (before the lazy import) to surface the friendly install hint via the established `OptionalDependencyError` path.'

findings.finding: The live end-to-end AC depends on the read layer from 'story E', which is a sibling story not yet delivered; this unacknowledged prerequisite means the AC's end-state-observable proof cannot be satisfied until story E ships.
findings.location: Scope — server.py ('one index route using the read layer from story E'); Acceptance Criteria — live end-to-end AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / dependencies' section noting 'Story E (audit read layer) must be merged first, OR the index route uses a stub that returns a hardcoded list for this story only, replaced when story E lands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an a11y done-definition to the index page scope: semantic HTML landmarks, keyboard-navigable links, sufficient color contrast (WCAG 2.1 AA 1.4.3), and screen-reader-compatible ticket list (WCAG 2.1 AA 1.3.1, 2.4.4).
evidence: 'a read-only INDEX page listing tickets that have audit data (each linking to a per-ticket page)'
ABSENCE: zero mention of WCAG, keyboard nav, screen reader, contrast, or reduced motion anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new user-facing HTML web surface (an index page rendered via Jinja2/FastAPI) with no mention of WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, keyboard navigation, screen-reader support, contrast requirements, or any other accessibility consideration.
findings.location: Scope / server.py ('one index route … read-only INDEX page listing tickets'); Acceptance Criteria AC 5
scenarios: The rendered HTML page is inaccessible to keyboard-only or screen-reader users; contrast ratios may fail WCAG 2.1 AA 1.4.3; ticket links may lack descriptive labels (WCAG 2.4.4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or a dedicated `docs/adr/` (or `docs/security.md`) entry recording the loopback-only / no-auth decision, its justification (datasette/mlflow precedent), and the explicit out-of-scope boundary for network exposure, so the decision survives in the repo after the ticket closes.
evidence: "The server binds 127.0.0.1 (loopback) ONLY and serves READ-ONLY content; there is no state-changing endpoint."
"Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit --host flag... actually exposing the service to a network (and any auth then required) is OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented."
ABSENCE: no ADR filename or docs/ section is named to capture this security decision
findings.finding: The loopback-only, no-auth security posture is a non-trivial architectural decision described inline in the plan but no ADR or dedicated doc artifact is named to record it as a durable, discoverable decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Section: 'Endpoint access contract (human/admin auth)'
scenarios: A future maintainer adds a --host option without realising the no-auth rationale, inadvertently exposing the service; the decision is only in the plan ticket, not in the repo's docs.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-audit-ui-loopback-noauth.md` (or append to an existing security ADR) capturing: decision = loopback-only + read-only → no auth required; rationale = local tooling precedent; future work = network exposure requires auth (out of scope). Reference the file from `docs/config.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a design-doc or ADR entry (e.g., `docs/decisions/audit-ui-no-auth.md`) capturing the loopback-as-auth-boundary posture, the no-network-principal guarantee, and the explicit out-of-scope boundary for non-loopback auth.
evidence: 'Human access control is the loopback boundary — no network principal can reach it — the justified no-auth posture (matching datasette / mlflow / git instaweb --local)'
No mention of ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
ABSENCE: no 'update docs/architecture.md' or 'add ADR' step in Scope or ACs
findings.finding: The loopback-as-auth-boundary posture is a novel architectural/security decision described inline in the plan but not committed to any design artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update).
findings.location: ## Endpoint access contract (human/admin auth)
scenarios: A future contributor adds a route that mutates state and doesn't realize the no-auth assumption; the decision was never formally recorded so it's invisible in code review.
A security reviewer audits the service and finds no documented rationale for the no-auth posture.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope line: 'Update `docs/decisions/` with an ADR for the loopback-only / no-auth posture, referencing datasette/mlflow precedent and documenting that non-loopback auth is out of scope for this epic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the fallback behavior for `rebar audit` (bare) and unknown subcommands — e.g. print usage/help and exit non-zero — and add a corresponding AC or oracle.
evidence: '_audit dispatches serve/show' — only two named dispatch arms, no else/fallback branch described
ABSENCE: no AC tests `rebar audit` (bare) or `rebar audit unknowncmd`
The intercept owns its own --help, implying it must also own its own error output
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show` but does not define behavior for `rebar audit` with no subcommand or an unrecognized subcommand, leaving the error path undefined.
findings.location: Scope — CLI: register `audit` as an INTERCEPT; `_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`
scenarios: `rebar audit` with no subcommand silently does nothing or crashes
A typo like `rebar audit sreve` produces a confusing error or traceback
findings.suggested_fix: In _audit(), add an else branch after dispatching serve/show: print a short usage string (or delegate to --help) and sys.exit(1). This mirrors the pattern used by other intercept commands in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (update `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS['audit']` one-liner to mention both `show` and `serve`) and `docs/cli-reference.md` (regenerate) to the Scope section as unenumerated consumers of the `audit` CLI surface change.
evidence: `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS['audit']` in `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (lines 40-44) currently reads: 'Show a ticket\'s audit trail: its full retained plan-review history ... (`audit show <ticket> [--output json|text]`).' — no mention of `serve`
`test.yml:294` and `gerrit-verify.yaml:250` run `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check` and fail the build on any diff
`tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` asserts `gen.main(['--check']) == 0`
Plan Scope section names no changes to `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` or `docs/cli-reference.md`
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` has a curated `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS['audit']` one-liner that only mentions `audit show <ticket>`. Adding `rebar audit serve` makes this description stale. A CI drift gate (`gen_cli_reference.py --check`) runs in both `test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml` and will fail if `docs/cli-reference.md` is not regenerated after updating the curated description. Neither `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` nor `docs/cli-reference.md` is named in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Scope section — CLI paragraph; scripts/gen_cli_reference.py:40-45; .github/workflows/test.yml:294
scenarios: Plan is implemented as written; the curated `audit` description is not updated; CI drift gate fails on `gen_cli_reference.py --check` (or the implementer regenerates without updating the description and the doc still omits `serve`)
`make test` fails at the `test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` assertion
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add: '`scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — update `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS["audit"]` to mention both `show` and `serve` subcommands; then regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` by running `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the human/admin auth posture for the non-loopback `--host` path: either (a) hard-reject the flag with a clear error in this story (deferring the flag itself to the epic that adds auth), or (b) name the auth mechanism that MUST be in place before non-loopback binding is allowed and document it in the plan.
evidence: "actually exposing the service to a network (and any auth then required) is OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented"
"Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit --host flag that prints a warning to stderr" — the flag is IN scope and the server does start
AC4: "rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0 prints a non-loopback exposure warning to stderr before binding" — binding still proceeds after the warning
No named identity mechanism (OIDC/OAuth/HTTP-auth/gateway) or hard rejection is specified for the non-loopback path
findings.finding: The plan permits binding a non-loopback address via `--host` (AC4 tests this path) but explicitly defers human/admin authentication for that exposure to 'OUT of scope', leaving no auth mechanism specified. The only guard is a stderr warning, which a user can ignore; the server then starts unauthenticated on a potentially untrusted network.
findings.location: 'Endpoint access contract' section; AC4; Scope §: `--host` flag
scenarios: A developer runs `rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` on a shared machine or CI runner, sees the warning, and the server starts anyway — all audit data is now accessible to any local-network principal with no authentication.
A script or CI job passes `--host` without reading stderr, silently exposing the service.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `--host` flag behaviour to a hard `SystemExit` with a message such as "Non-loopback binding is not supported until network auth is implemented; see <issue>" rather than a warning-and-continue. The warning-and-continue model can be introduced in the story that actually implements the auth layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit access class for the `--host <non-loopback>` path (e.g., 'public-unauthenticated-by-design, no PII guaranteed' OR 'operator must add a reverse-proxy with auth/TLS; this flag is for advanced use only') and document whether plain HTTP is acceptable on that path — the current 'out of scope' deferral leaves the posture undeclared, which is the T5c gap.
evidence: "Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit `--host` flag that prints a warning to stderr; actually exposing the service to a network (and any auth then required) is OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented."
AC: "`rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` prints a non-loopback exposure warning to stderr before binding" — the server still starts and serves after the warning, with no declared access class
ABSENCE: no declaration of whether the non-loopback path is 'public-unauthenticated-by-design', 'internal-network-only', or 'must-not-be-used-without-auth'; the plan only says auth is 'out of scope'
ABSENCE: no mention of TLS/HTTPS requirement or prohibition when the server is bound to a non-loopback interface
findings.finding: The `--host` flag allows binding to a non-loopback address (e.g., 0.0.0.0), which opens a trust boundary to lower-trust network actors. The plan defers auth to 'out of scope' but does not declare an explicit access class (public-unauthenticated-by-design / authenticated / internal-network-only) for this boundary-crossing path, and does not address the plaintext-HTTP exposure of audit data over the network. An undeclared access posture on a reachable path is the T5c failure, regardless of whether auth is ultimately required.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; AC item 4 (`--host` warning); Scope section (`server.py` `serve(host, port)`)
scenarios: A developer runs `rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` on a shared machine or cloud VM — audit findings become readable by any user on the same network, with no auth and no TLS, over plain HTTP.
A CI or container environment forwards ports unexpectedly; the warning is printed to stderr (likely lost in logs) and the server starts anyway, silently exposing audit data.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Endpoint access contract' section, add a sentence declaring the non-loopback access class explicitly, e.g.: 'When `--host` is set to a non-loopback address, the server is intentionally unauthenticated and HTTP-only; the operator assumes full responsibility for network isolation and is advised to place a reverse proxy with TLS and auth in front. This posture is declared public-no-auth-by-design for operator convenience; no PII guarantees are made.' Then update AC-4 to assert that the warning message includes this posture statement (not just a generic warning), so the operator sees the declared class at runtime.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
